front: Manuscript: held at the British Library; its sole surviving copy of Beowulf has one page showing heavy wear attributed to reader interest in the dragon section
work: Beowulf (as contained in the Nowell Codex)

front: Manuscript: central section contains characters Onela, Heardred, Hygelac, and Wiglaf
work: Beowulf (as contained in the Nowell Codex)

front: Manuscript: Grimur Jonsson Thorkelin transcribed Beowulf from this manuscript in 1787
work: Beowulf (as contained in the Nowell Codex)

front: Manuscript: contains the fragmentary 9th-century Old English poem Judith, described as being about a 'strong servant of the Savior'
Poem: the heroine commands her maidservant to take a head from a food sack and show it to the people of Bethulia
Poem: the protagonist's taking of Holofernes's head mirrors Beowulf's taking of Grendel's head
Manuscript: contains the illustrated prose text Wonders of the East, depicting fantastic peoples such as blemmyae and hostes
work: Wonders of the East (illustrated prose in the Nowell Codex)

front: Manuscript: contains a letter from Alexander the Great to Aristotle alongside the Wonders of the East
work: Wonders of the East (illustrated prose in the Nowell Codex)

front: Manuscript: named after its first known owner, Laurence Nowell
Manuscript: also known as Cotton MS Vitellius A.XV; this codex forms the second part of that collection
Manuscript: created around the early 11th century (circa year 1000)
Manuscript: edges of its pages were scorched in the 1731 Ashburnham House fire
Manuscript: first described by Franciscus Junius; later studied by Kevin Kiernan using UV light
Manuscript: Kemp Malone improved Nikolaj Grundtvig's reconstructions of text from its fire-scorched edges
Manuscript: written by two scribes; the first stopped mid-sentence and was replaced by a scribe who spelled a name with 'I' instead of 'E'
Manuscript: subject of Andy Orchard's book Pride and Prodigies
